headline: KWAP Pension Fund Returns Lag EPF by 1.8% Over 5 Years
context: The Retirement Fund Inc (KWAP) managing RM172 billion in civil servant pension assets delivered 5-year annualised returns of 3.6% versus EPF's 5.4%, raising concerns about the management of public sector retirement savings.

cards.big: KWAP's RM172 billion returned only 3.6% annually — 1.8% below EPF — costing civil servant pensions RM3.1 billion per year
cards.sub: 5-year underperformance compounds to RM18 billion in foregone returns — money that should fund retirements

cards.big: KWAP's domestic equity allocation at 48% is concentrated in GLCs that themselves underperform the broader Bursa index
cards.sub: GLC-heavy portfolio creates circular dependency — KWAP props up GLC valuations while GLCs deliver mediocre returns to KWAP

cards.big: 1.7 million civil servants rely on KWAP-funded defined benefit pensions — underperformance threatens long-term sustainability
cards.sub: Pension obligation growing at 6.2% annually while KWAP returns are 3.6% — actuarial deficit widening every year

cards.big: KWAP board has 6 government representatives and 2 independents — investment decisions influenced by national policy objectives rather than returns
cards.sub: Unlike EPF, KWAP publishes no detailed portfolio performance breakdown — civil servants cannot see how their pension money performs

cards.big: Using pension money to support government policy goals instead of maximising returns cheats civil servants out of the retirement they earned
Should KWAP be required to match EPF's returns or face mandatory board restructuring with majority independent directors?
